straight_beat.name: Delmar Fontenot
straight_beat.column:
The Astros lost again, but not in a way that feels entirely inevitable. Yesterday’s 9-1 drubbing of the Rockies was a brutal reminder of how fragile this team’s balance can be. You don’t need a chalkboard to see it — the numbers are screaming. Houston’s 6-7 record, a 1.5-game deficit in the AL West, isn’t just a stat; it’s a tape measure of it. The question isn’t whether they’re winning or losing — it’s how they’re doing it, and how they’re not.  

Framber Valdez started like a man who’d just finished a marathon. Six innings, 100 pitches, a line that looked decent on paper: 5.1 innings, 5 runs, 5 walks. But Valdez isn’t the problem. The problem is the lineup that failed to capitalize on his labor. The Rockies’ offense didn’t just outslug Houston — they outmanned them. Monfort’s line drive, Arraez’s double, and the Rockies’ ability to string hits together felt like a script written by someone who’s seen too many highlight reels. Houston’s bats, meanwhile, looked like a group of players who’ve forgotten how

optimist.name: Luz Barquero
optimist.column:
The Astros lost yesterday, but let’s not let that cloud the bigger picture. Sure, a 9-1 drubbing stings, but you know what doesn’t? The fact that these guys are still out there swinging, pitching, and chasing the same dream. Losing streaks? Nah, that’s just the boys stretching. Bregman’s swing is one at-bat away from April flipping into June.  

Now, I’ll be the first to admit the last few weeks haven’t been pretty. The Rockies handed them a lesson in patience, and let’s be real—some of those runs weren’t exactly earned. But here’s the thing: every game is a chance to reset. You can’t control the other team’s lineup, but you can control how you approach the next one. And that’s where the real magic happens.  

Look, the Astros aren’t perfect. They’re still figuring out how to consistently string wins together, and that’s okay. You don’t build a contender overnight, especially when you’ve got a roster that’s still finding its rhythm. But here’s what I’ve noticed lately: the heart. The way they keep grinding, even when the scoreboard isn’t lighting

pessimist.name: Coop Yarbrough
pessimist.column:
The Astros lost again. Not just lost, but lost in a way that feels inevitable, like a punchline to a joke that’s been told for 30 years. Yesterday’s 9-1 drubbing by Colorado wasn’t just another loss—it was a reminder that the weight of the 2017 asterisk hasn’t lifted. You can’t unhear it. It’s in the dugout, in the coaching staff’s meetings, in the way the ball seems to slip through their fingers every time they try to catch up.  

The record now reads 6-7, but that’s not the number that haunts me. It’s the 1.5 games behind in the AL West, the gap that feels wider than it should be. You look at the roster and wonder: What’s the plan here? Are they still trying to rebuild, or are they just waiting for the front office to admit they’ve been chasing shadows since 2017? The same front office that once traded away prospects for a chance at a championship, now seems content to trade away hope for a few more draft picks. Rigged from the jump, right?  

The problem isn’t just the front office. It’s the team’s inability to stop the bleeding. Every clean hit they get, I hear a trash can. Can’t unhear it. Nobody can. That’s the punishment and the crime
